query: What are the trends in workforce jobs in London?

response.headline: London’s Job Numbers Have Soared by Over a Third Since 2005
response.lede: More people are working in London now than ever before. Over the past twenty years, the number of jobs has jumped by about a third. In 2005, the city had just under 4.7 million workforce jobs; by late 2025, that number had climbed to over 6.4 million.

story_blocks.body: London has added nearly 2 million jobs in the last two decades.

story_blocks.heading: London’s Workforce Has Grown Steadily for 20 Years
story_blocks.body: In the chart below, you’ll see a line showing job numbers in London from 2005 to 2025. The line is on an upward path almost the whole way, with only minor dips. The steady rise means London has managed to add jobs year after year, reflecting the city’s economic growth. Today, job opportunities are more plentiful than ever before.

story_blocks.heading: London’s Share of Jobs Compared to the Rest of the UK
story_blocks.body: The bar chart below compares workforce jobs in London and the whole UK for the most recent data. London’s job total is much smaller than the UK as a whole, but it has grown faster—up 37% since 2005, compared to 17% nationwide. The chart helps put London’s numbers in context, showing the scale of opportunity here relative to the rest of the country.

story_blocks.heading: London’s Growing Role in the UK Job Market
story_blocks.body: In the third chart, you can see how London’s share of the UK’s total workforce jobs has gradually increased over time. This means the capital is contributing more to national employment than it did twenty years ago. For anyone living or working here, this means more options—and more competition—in the job market.

story_blocks.heading: What This Means for Residents
story_blocks.body: If you’re looking for work or thinking about changing jobs, the outlook in London is strong. There are simply more jobs today than there were a generation ago. That ongoing growth can mean more opportunities, but it also means staying prepared for changes in the city’s fast-moving job market.

response.data_note: These numbers cover March 2005 to September 2025 and are updated every few months. The data captures all types of jobs—employees, self-employed, and others. Figures are rounded to the nearest thousand. While the source is from the Office for National Statistics, licence information could not be confirmed, and if you live in a particular neighbourhood, trends there may differ. The data looks at London as a whole.

description.value:
Workforce Jobs (WFJ) is a quarterly measure of the number of jobs in the UK and is the preferred measure of the change in jobs by industry.

It is a compound source that draws on a range of employer surveys, household surveys and administrative sources. WFJ is the sum of employee jobs measured primarily by employer surveys, self-employment jobs from the Labour Force Survey, and government-supported trainees and Her Majesty's Forces from administrative sources.

The spreadsheet shows change since last quarter and last year, with graphs displaying these trends.

Data is available by industry from NOMIS WEB. Data back to Sep 1981 available for the UK, and back to March 1996 for UK regions (included in spreadsheet).

Seasonally adjusted. The figures in this dataset are adjusted to compensate for seasonal variations in employment.

UK totals include overseas based HM Forces personnel that cannot be assigned to a region.

Supporting information is now available to identify a small number of estimates that are deemed unreliable from https://www.nomisweb.co.uk/articles/654.aspx.

Data is rounded to the nearest 1000.

Relevant link: http://www.nomisweb.co.uk/

For Borough level modelled estimates and projections of jobs see the GLA Employment Projections.

Note - previously published figures may differ slightly from those published in December 2024 due to revisions made by Nomis. Find more information here: https://www.nomisweb.co.uk/articles/1414.aspx?utm_source
